title: Ridge Regression (L2)
concept: Ridge regression, or L2 regularization, penalizes the sum of squared weights, shrinking them close to zero but keeping all features.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why do we want to penalize large weights in standard regression?

options.text: To keep the model stable and prevent it from overfitting to training noise
To ensure the model only uses a single feature
To guarantee that the training error becomes exactly zero

exercises.explain: Excellent! Penalizing large weights prevents overfitting and keeps predictions stable. Now, let's look at Ridge Regression, which does exactly this by using squared weights.

cards.title: The gentle pull
cards.body: Imagine standing on a moving train. If you stand too stiffly, you'll fall over when the train bumps. You need a gentle, centering force that keeps you balanced without locking you in place.

cards.title: What is Ridge Regression?
cards.body: Ridge Regression (also called L2 regularization) adds a penalty to the cost function equal to the sum of the squared weights, forcing the model to keep all weights small.

cards.title: Deflating balloons
cards.body: Imagine your model's weights are helium balloons of different sizes. L2 regularization acts like a cooling mist that shrinks all the balloons slightly. They all get smaller, but none of them disappear completely.

cards.title: The squared penalty
cards.body: If weights are 3 and 4, the L2 penalty is proportional to 3² + 4² = 25. If the weights double to 6 and 8, the penalty quadruples to 36 + 64 = 100! This heavy scaling stops weights from growing large.

cards.title: Do weights hit zero?
cards.body: You might expect L2 regularization to shrink useless weights all the way to zero. In reality, because the penalty gets tiny when the weight is small, weights get very close to zero, but never exactly zero.

cards.title: L2 Penalty vs. No Penalty
cards.body: How L2 regularization (Ridge) affects model weights:

exercises.prompt: What mathematical term does Ridge (L2) regression add to the cost function?

options.text: The sum of the squared weights
The sum of the absolute values of the weights
The square root of the number of features
The average of all feature values

exercises.explain: Ridge regression adds a penalty proportional to the sum of the squared weights. This squared penalty is why it is called L2 regularization.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Ridge regression can shrink weights to be extremely small, but it never sets them to exactly zero.
exercises.explain: True! Because the L2 penalty shrinks weights proportionally, a weight gets smaller and smaller but never quite touches absolute zero.

exercises.sentence: Ridge regression is also commonly known as ___ regularization.
exercises.explain: L2 regularization is the technical name for penalizing the sum of squared weights, which is the core of Ridge regression.

exercises.prompt: Which of these weight lists is a typical result of applying Ridge (L2) regularization to a model with weights that started as [10.0, -8.0, 5.0]?
exercises.explain: Ridge shrinks all weights towards zero while keeping them all non-zero, resulting in values like [0.15, -0.12, 0.08].

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict sales using 100 features. You know that almost all features have a tiny bit of useful signal, and you want to prevent overfitting. Which technique should you choose?

options.text: Ridge (L2) regression, because it shrinks weights to be small but keeps all 100 features in the model
Standard linear regression with no penalty
A method that drops 99 features entirely
A simple average of historical sales

exercises.explain: Perfect! Since every feature has some signal, Ridge is ideal. It shrinks the weights to prevent overfitting while retaining every single feature. 🎉
